message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Patients with IBD are considered immunosuppressed, but do not seem more vulnerable for COVID-19. Nevertheless, intestinal inflammation has shown an important risk factor for SARS-CoV-2 infection and prognosis. Therefore, we investigated the effect of intestinal inflammation on the viral intestinal entry mechanisms, including
                  <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                  , in IBD.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>We collected (un)inflamed mucosal biopsies from CD (n=193) and UC (n=158) patients, and 51 matched non-IBD controls for RNA sequencing, differential gene expression and co-expression analysis. Organoids from UC patients were subjected to an inflammatory mix and processed for RNA sequencing. Transmural ileal biopsies were processed for single-cell (sc) sequencing. Publicly available colonic sc-RNA sequencing data, and microarrays from tissue pre/post anti-TNF therapy, were analyzed.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  In inflamed CD ileum,
                  <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                  was significantly decreased compared to control ileum (p=4.6E-07), whereas colonic
                  <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                  expression was higher in inflamed colon of CD/UC compared to control (p=8.3E-03; p=1.9E-03). Sc-RNA sequencing confirmed this
                  <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                  dysregulation, and exclusive epithelial
                  <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                  expression. Network analyses highlighted
                  <jats:italic>HNF4A</jats:italic>
                  as key regulator of ileal
                  <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                  , while pro-inflammatory cytokines and interferon regulating factors regulated colonic
                  <jats:italic>ACE2.</jats:italic>
                  Inflammatory stimuli upregulated
                  <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                  in UC organoids (p=1.7E-02), not in non-IBD controls (p=9.1E-01). Anti-TNF therapy restored colonic
                  <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                  dysregulation in responders.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  Intestinal inflammation alters SARS-CoV-2 coreceptors in the intestine, with opposing effects in ileum and colon.
                  <jats:italic>HNF4A</jats:italic>
                  , an IBD susceptibility gene, is an important upstream regulator of
                  <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                  in ileum, whereas interferon signaling dominates in colon. Our data support the importance of adequate control of IBD in order to reduce risk of (complicated) COVID-19.
                </jats:p>
title: Intestinal receptor of SARS-CoV-2 in inflamed IBD tissue is downregulated by HNF4A in ileum and upregulated by interferon regulating factors in colon
